text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'll share how I automate non-coding tasks with Claude Code. So, you've probably heard of Claude Code and how developers are using it to build software faster. While coding is Claude Code's biggest use case, it's not its only use case. That's because we can think of Claude Code as a general-purpose agent that is deployed where you work. In other words, on your computer. And the reason it's a general-purpose agent is because all it is is Anthropic's Claude given a handful of key tools, such as the ability to read and write files and folders on your computer, the ability to search the web, generate and run programs on your computer, come up with plans and to-do lists to get things done. And finally, the ability to spin up sub agents to help it accomplish goals. Using these tools, Claude Code can do many non-coding tasks, such as doing sales pre-call deep dives. Just give it access to the right data, and then it'll be able to write code to analyze your data and generate visualizations. So, you might be thinking, "Why do I need to use Claude Code for this? Can't I use a tool like ChatGPT or Gemini or the like?" And while you could do much of this stuff with other applications and models, there are three key reasons why I personally prefer Claude Code for automating work. The first reason is that right now Claude is the best model out there for tool calling. Tool calling is this idea of expanding the AI beyond just generating text. It consists of giving it access to the right tools, like web search, a code interpreter, access to your file system, your email account, access to your Notion, access to an external database, and on and on and on, so that it can be more helpful. And the types of sophisticated tool calls that Claude can do right now just feels way beyond what I see other models doing. The second reason is Claude Code gives you more visibility and control over the tools and context that are being passed to Claude. Even though everything that I'm going to talk about in this video can easily be applied to Claude's default web interface, Claude Code just allows you to get that next level of customization and steerability. And the last reason to use Claude Code is that it's deployed directly where you work. So, if you're using Claude in the browser, it's not going to have access to your files and folders and your local applications, but if you're using Claude Code and it's running on your computer, it's going to be able to access all the files and tools that you're already using. So far, we've talked about what Claude Code is and why we should use it, but how do we use this tool to actually automate everything? The framework that I'm going to talk about in this video is built on top of two key ingredients. The first is MCP, which gives Claude access to external tools. The way I think about this is MCP gives Claude capabilities. It gives it the ability to connect to your Notion account or your Gmail account or the ability to answer questions about data analytics dashboards or get context about your organic marketing campaigns or be able to review code or review new tickets on Jira. But just because Claude has the capability to do something, that doesn't necessarily mean that it's able to execute specific tasks particularly well. That brings up the second ingredient, which are so-called skills. These give Claude competence. In other words, they teach Claude how to use its tools, whether it's the default ones or additional ones you add with MCP, well. Skills are simply a bundle of text files and folders that Claude can access as needed. So every skill will have a folder with a unique name and each of those folders there'll be this skill.md file, which is just a text file, and then optionally you can add additional text files or you can add folders in this skill folder to give Claude additional context. And the key feature of skills is this idea of so-called progressive disclosure, which is just a fancy way of saying that skills give Claude the right context at the right time. So instead of just dumping all of this information on Claude at the start of every conversation, skills work in the following way, where you'll have your skills library and the first thing that Claude will see is a short description of what each skill does and when to use them. Then, if Claude decides a particular skill is useful, it's going to load the entire skill.md file into its context. Then, as the conversation continues, Claude can optionally add additional files from this skill folder into the context window whenever it sees fit. The second way is you can add MCP servers in the terminal. Typically, whoever created the MCP server will have installation instructions. And finally, there is a plugins feature in Claude code, which makes this process a bit more streamlined. If you run {slash} plugin in your Claude code session, you'll see a handful of out-of-the-box bundles of MCP servers and other things that you can easily add to your Claude code account. So, to get started here, I would just recommend adding the one or two tools that you use most often. And then to check out all the MCP servers you have running in your Claude code session, you can run this {slash} MCP command, and there you can easily enable or disable any of the MCP servers you have installed. However, just because Claude has the capability to read my emails or create a notion page, that doesn't necessarily mean it has the competence to do specific tasks well. Actually, most of this framework for automating non-coding tasks relies on this second ingredient of skills to ensure that Claude is actually helpful for getting work done. There are also three ways we can add skills to Claude code. The first manually, so you can find some skill file on the internet that you trust, and you can add it to your projects.claude folder, or you can add it to your user-level Claude folder. However, what I usually do is I just ask Claude. So, if I find a skill like Anthropic's skill creator skill, I'll just grab the GitHub URL for whatever skill I want to add, and give it to Claude and ask it to add it to either my user level skills or my project level skills. Finally, you can also use plugins. Plugins aren't just MCP servers, they're actually bundles of MCP servers, skills, sub agents, and Claude code automations called hooks. So, this is an easy way to explore MCP servers and skills without going on the internet. While grabbing off-the-shelf skills is a easy way to get started with them, the real value in unlock is when you start creating your own custom skills for your unique personal workflows. This is really the most powerful way you can get Claude code to automate work for you because rather than using a generic skill available off-the-shelf, you're now teaching Claude all the nuances of your particular work and allowing it to get better over time. The workflow I use for this goes as follows. I'll start with a specific request. Let's say I want Claude to prioritize emails in my inbox for me. I'll just send that request to Claude code. Then, there'll be a long conversation of Claude exploring my Gmail account, calling tools, making mistakes, getting feedback from me, using more tools, thinking a bit longer, and on and on and on. So, there might be a lot of back and forth here to finally get Claude to do what I want, but eventually Claude will complete the task successfully, even if it took like 30 minutes or so. But, then the next step is to synthesize everything Claude did and learned during that conversation into a skill. So, what this might look like is I'll just tell Claude, "Use the skills creator to create a new skill about what we just did." And then, Claude will automatically reflect on everything that it did over the past 30 minutes or so and compress it into a skill. And I typically like to do this on plan mode because it's important that the skill is aligned with the way I want the task to be done. So, I don't want Claude to just write the skill in one shot. I want it to present how it's thinking about writing the skill and allow me to give it feedback on its reasoning. So, sometimes I might go back and forth on Claude's plan for writing the skill until actually writing the initial version. But then from here, even though we have a skill, we don't really know if it works well. So, the next step is to test it on other requests. Often I'll just do one additional task, or maybe even the same exact task that I just did, but doing it from scratch. And what should happen is instead of it taking 30 minutes, Claude should be able to do it in one shot or with at least less feedback than you gave it initially. And then every time you have to give Claude feedback or it runs into an error or it takes way too long to do something, that's an opportunity for improvement. So, you can have Claude reflect on any mistakes or feedback or learnings and update the skill accordingly. Then you can run more experiments and then just continue this iterative loop. What this means is skills aren't these fixed rigid things, but rather they are these adaptive instructions that can improve every time you use them. The power of skills is that they enable so-called in-context continual learning. So, that's just a fancy way of saying the more you use the skill, the smarter Claude gets. And the mechanism for this is every time Claude uses a skill and then makes a mistake or writes custom code or runs into an unexpected error or takes a very long time to do something, this is an opportunity for improvement. So, at the end of one of these conversations, and Claude has successfully done the task, You can say something like, "That took you a really long time." Or you made a lot of different mistakes, or a few unexpected errors came up. How can the skill be updated so that you can do this faster next time? While your initial conversation with Claude to do something complicated may take 30 minutes or so, once you have a good skill, this time can get cut down to just 30 seconds. And then once Claude can do something well, maybe you want to expand the scope of the different types of things that Claude can do in that context. So far, this has been pretty high-level and abstract, so let's make this more concrete with three real-world use cases. Here are three recent non-coding tasks that I've automated using the ingredients and framework I described so far. The first is automating cold outreach, the second is creating slide decks with Keynote, finally writing three-way intro emails. The first use case is all about cold outreach, so finding prospects, validating their emails, and drafting emails to them. The way I did this is as follows. I started by just having Claude research leaders at local, mid-sized, PE-backed technology companies. This research probably took about 20 or 30 minutes, and I had Claude get the name of the contact, the name of their company, the head count of the company, their email, and then a short description of that person, [snorts] their company, and why they might be worth reaching out to for my AI training services. From there, I took the leads and I put them into a Notion database. So, I kept the person's name, their contact email. I had a segment for them, so this was a experiment, wanted to see how good Claude's lead list was. And then I had a tag for the company size. All I pasted in was the name and contact, but then I had Claude automatically look at the original CSV file and add this company size tag automatically, so I didn't have to do it manually. The reason this is possible is because Notion has a MCP server that allows Claude or any other AI assistant to read, edit, and write changes to your Notion pages. However, when I tried to start sending some emails, it seemed about 10% of the emails were just made up. This is a well-known problem with LLM, sometimes they make things up. So, I had this third step of having Claude go through each row of this Notion database and validate the email using Apollo's MCP server. Apollo is a service for data enrichment. They have a massive database of professionals, their roles, their companies, their contact information, and so on. So, I used it to validate all the emails in this database. And then finally, once all the emails were validated, I had an outreach script in a different Notion page, and I just told Claude to grab that script and then craft a personalized email for every single person in this database and save it as a draft to my Gmail account. So, instead of me having to manually copy-paste 50 emails or so and potentially make a mistake like adding the wrong name to the email or whatever it might be, Claude was able to do this in seconds. From here, I reviewed each email and hit send manually. This experiment is still ongoing. I don't have the results of this outreach campaign. However, from a automation perspective, it's very exciting because just by giving Claude access to the right tools of my Notion account, my Apollo account, and my Gmail account, it's able to fully automate a very time-consuming process. If I were to try to research leads manually and then organize them into a notion database and then validate the emails with Apollo and then write and send emails for all of these leads, especially if I'm reaching out to hundreds, if not thousands of people, this would take up a significant amount of time. But potentially, if this is helpful, I can automate this whole pipeline and potentially have more customers than I know what to do with. Finally, a script that allows it to extract all the text from a particular slide. This is what the skill file looks like. This is the standard format for skills. There's going to be this so-called front matter part, which is basically metadata. Here you'll have the skill name and a short description of what it is and when to use it. Then below, you'll essentially have a specialized prompt that tells Claude what the skill is and give it key pieces of instruction. But, if it does make a mistake, that's great because then I can go back to Claude and say, "Hey, you made this mistake here. Update the skill so you don't do this in the future." We covered a lot of ground in this video, so I just wanted to wrap up with a few key takeaways. The first is that Claude code isn't just a coding agent. We can use it to automate non-coding tasks like doing sales outreach, building keynote presentations, and automating email introductions like the use cases I covered here. What enables this are these two key ingredients of MCP servers and skills, which enable adaptive no-code automations with Claude. MCP gives Claude the capability to do something, so it gives it access to the right tools so it can potentially do a task. While skills give Claude competence so that not only can it do the task after 30 minutes of back and forth, but it can do the task well and on a single try. We also talked about how skills allow Claude to get better every time you use it by giving it feedback anytime it makes a mistake or runs into errors or takes longer than it should have taken to accomplish a task. Finally, while this process might be slow at first, these improvements will compound on one another. So, even though the first time you automate it, it's going to take longer than if you were to just do it yourself, over time, the savings are going to grow and your skills are going to become more and more sophisticated, taking even more work off your plate. So, developing these automations is really an investment and it won't really make sense for one-off tasks or for work that you really don't do that often. It's going to give you the greatest value for work that you're doing over and over again that are the most draining or the most time-consuming. Those are going to be the best opportunities. So, I hope this video was helpful in giving you a flavor of what's possible today with Cloud Code.
